actions_taken: REORDER SKU_1 from backup SUP_B|REROUTE via R4
reflection_notes: Switching to backup supplier with priority reorder prevented stockout within 48h. High cost but acceptable service level maintained.

actions_taken: REROUTE all SKUs from R3 to R1|REORDER SKU_2 safety buffer
reflection_notes: Rerouting early before stockout reduced risk significantly. Building safety buffer for high-demand SKUs proved effective.

actions_taken: REORDER SKU_2 x50 emergency|SWITCH_SUPPLIER to high-capacity SUP_D
reflection_notes: Demand spike required immediate large reorder. Switching to high-capacity supplier avoided stockout but at higher unit cost.

actions_taken: REORDER from primary supplier with extended lead time buffer
reflection_notes: Moderate delay handled by increasing order quantity from primary. No supplier switch needed — cost-effective resolution.

actions_taken: SWITCH_SUPPLIER all SKUs|REROUTE via secondary routes|REORDER emergency buffer x2
reflection_notes: Compound disruption required full mobilization. Despite high cost service level held above 80%. Prior early warning improved response time.

actions_taken: REROUTE SKU_1 to backup route|monitor only
reflection_notes: Low-severity blockage resolved by simple reroute. No inventory action needed. Fast recovery at minimal cost.

actions_taken: REORDER all SKUs x2|SWITCH_SUPPLIER to SUP_B|add buffer stock
reflection_notes: Extreme demand spike required multi-SKU emergency reorder. Margin compressed but service level held.

actions_taken: EMERGENCY SWITCH_SUPPLIER|expedite REORDER|REROUTE to fastest route
reflection_notes: Critical supplier failure at peak season. Required full emergency protocol. Cost was very high but stockout was avoided.
Matched common route blockage pattern. Rerouting SKU_3 early is the standard successful play.
